video_title: War Is Back. Saylor Sold. Bitcoin Went Up Anyway?
gist: Bankless weekly rollup (July 2026): renewed US-Iran strikes barely moved oil or crypto, showing the market has desensitized to the war; Strategy sold 3,588 BTC yet Bitcoin rose, raising the hosts' odds that ~$57.5k was the cycle bottom. Also covers Robinhood chain's memecoin-driven launch, Vitalik's new dated Ethereum 'straw map' (framed as an app-chain for ETH as store of value), JP Morgan's $700M tokenized money-market fund on Ethereum L1, and the emerging Hyperliquid-vs-Lighter perp-DEX rivalry.

insights.claim: Renewed US airstrikes on Iran (roughly 170 military sites over July 8-9, retaliation for Iran attacking three commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz) pushed WTI crude only from about $68 to $71, roughly 5%, which the hosts frame as negligible in the context of an active war.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Geopolitical escalation is no longer a reliable oil/risk-off catalyst; fade knee-jerk war-driven oil spikes.
insights.quote: oil prices jumped from $68 for WTI up to $71. That's up 5%. which is in the grand scheme of the war. That's like nothing.

insights.claim: During the war's peak the Trump administration conditioned markets by striking only on weekends and keeping weekday market hours 'peacetime,' which the hosts credit for the market now shrugging off fresh strikes on Iran.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Expectation-management cadence has trained markets to ignore the conflict; volatility from headlines is likely to stay muted.
insights.quote: they only struck on the weekends. Then it was like peace time between Monday through Friday during market hours.

insights.claim: The hosts argue equity/oil markets, not Iran, are the true constraint on Trump's military action: because a two-day strike campaign barely dented markets, Trump faces little pressure to de-escalate.
insights.speaker: Ryan (host)
insights.so_what: War-risk pricing should track market drawdowns rather than diplomatic rhetoric; watch equities as the real 'off switch.'
insights.quote: Donald Trump gets to do whatever he wants cuz the market is what will push Donald into a corner, not Iran.

insights.claim: Strategy (Michael Saylor) sold 3,588 Bitcoin this week for about $216 million, its first sale of size and roughly half a percent of holdings, yet Bitcoin still rose ~2-3% on the week.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: The market absorbing a marquee forced seller without dropping is a bullish structural signal for BTC.
insights.quote: Sailor sold 3,588 Bitcoin and Bitcoin went up 3%.

insights.claim: The $216M Saylor raised covers only about 45 days (roughly 1.5 months) of Strategy's preferred-dividend obligations, so the sale buys little time and implies a recurring cadence of further BTC sales is coming.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Expect repeated orderly Strategy BTC sales; treat them as a known overhang rather than a shock.
insights.quote: He raised $216 million, which is like one and a half months of dividend coverage.

insights.claim: David argues it is bullish for Saylor to sell because he needs to become 'irrelevant to the market' by clearing his dividend overhang, and that Saylor is still not selling enough to secure 30-plus months of coverage.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Removing the Saylor overhang is a precondition for BTC to trade on its own fundamentals; larger orderly sales would be a positive.
insights.quote: he needs to become in my opinion irrelevant to the market

insights.claim: The hosts contend Bitcoin's earlier ~20% drop (roughly $70k to $58k) after Saylor sold just 32 BTC was the market front-running large future sales like this one, not a reaction to the tiny sale itself.
insights.speaker: Ryan (host)
insights.so_what: If the anticipated selling was already priced in, actual orderly sales may relieve rather than add pressure.
insights.quote: It was the market anticipating much larger sells like this coming in the future.

insights.claim: David raised his probability that Bitcoin's cycle bottom is already in to about 60%, pinpointing ~$57,500 as the likely bottom, precisely because Saylor was revealed to be the forced seller into that $57k level.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: A named forced seller marking the low strengthens the case that $57.5k held as the bottom; a level to accumulate against.
insights.quote: Had I known that Sailor was actually selling into that, I'd be like, "Oh, he's the forced seller selling into the bottom. This is what a bottom looks like."

insights.claim: Analyst Michael Nato puts only ~45% odds the Bitcoin bottom is in, has staggered limit buy orders at ~$55k, then $50k, then into the $40s, and notes this bottom lacked the heavy capitulation volume seen at prior violent bottoms.
insights.speaker: Ryan (host)
insights.so_what: Absence of capitulation volume argues for lower lows; watch for a high-volume flush before trusting the bottom.
insights.quote: we haven't seen the volume that we typically see in these kind of mark the bottom capitulation type events

insights.claim: A Bitcoin move to ~$40k would represent roughly a 64% drawdown from all-time highs, which the hosts note is consistent with prior cycle drawdown magnitudes.
insights.speaker: Ryan (host)
insights.so_what: Sizing downside risk: $40k is a historically plausible worst-case entry zone, not an outlier.

insights.claim: Michael Nato flipped from ~80% cash just before the 'October 10' drop to about 70% crypto / 30% cash now, signaling a convicted re-entry ahead of the Bankless hosts.
insights.speaker: Ryan (host)
insights.so_what: A previously cash-heavy cycle analyst going 70% long is a positioning signal that smart money is buying the range.

insights.claim: Strategy's disclosed monetization framework has three buckets: a $1.25B 'build the reserve' authorization, a limitless 'cover the preferreds' bucket, and a 'fund buybacks' bucket of up to $1B each for repurchasing preferreds and MSTR stock.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: The 'cover the preferreds' bucket is uncapped, so Strategy can legally sell unlimited BTC to service dividends.
insights.quote: It's in the cover the preferred bucket which is an limitless bucket. They can sell as much Bitcoin as they need to cover the preferred.

insights.claim: This week's Strategy Bitcoin sale came from the uncapped 'cover the preferreds' bucket and did not touch the separate $1.25B 'build the reserve' authorization, which remains fully available.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Reserve-selling capacity is untouched on top of unlimited preferred-coverage selling, implying more potential supply overhang.

insights.claim: Strategy's preferred instrument STRC ('stretch') is trading around $86, about 14% off, which David reads as the market finding an equilibrium where $100 is too high and $70 too low.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: STRC's price is a real-time gauge of confidence in Strategy's dividend funding; watch the $70-$100 band.
insights.quote: $100 is too high, but $70 is too low.

insights.claim: Robinhood chain's first full production week was driven not by tokenized stocks but by memecoins, led by 'Cash Cat' (based on Robinhood's original name), which spiked to roughly $180 million market cap and drained bridge ETH liquidity.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Memecoins remain the default bootstrapping mechanism for new chains; RWA-focused chains still launch on speculative activity.
insights.quote: It's called Cash Cat and the lore is that this is what Robin Hood was called before it was called Robin Hood.

insights.claim: Robinhood chain surpassed 200,000 cumulative wallets with about 140,000 new active wallets in a single day (July 8) and did $500M of Uniswap volume in 24 hours, roughly a third of Solana's DEX spot volume.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Robinhood chain is arguably the most successful L2 launch of the past ~12 months, a bullish datapoint for the Ethereum L2 thesis.
insights.quote: $500 million of trading volume in unis swap on in 24 hours, which is roughly a third of Salana's DEX spot volume

insights.claim: Of Robinhood chain's ~$366M total assets, ~$266M is stablecoins with ~$90M in Ethena and ~$86M in Morpho earning yield (7% boosted by Paxos' USG stablecoin), while only ~$13M sits in tokenized Robinhood stocks.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Early RWA/tokenized-stock demand is tiny versus yield-farming stablecoins; Morpho and Ethena are the real near-term beneficiaries.
insights.quote: there is a whopping $13 million of tokenized uh stocks Robin Hood tokenized stocks $13 million

insights.claim: Arbitrum (ARB) rose 13% on the week because it receives 10% of all Robinhood chain execution fees, making it the direct token beneficiary of that chain's activity.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: ARB is a levered play on Robinhood chain throughput via its fee share; track RH chain volume for ARB.
insights.quote: they get 10% of all of the execution fees of Robin Hood chain goes into the arbitr

insights.claim: Robinhood chain has spent only about $600 buying Ethereum L1 blob/data-availability space so far, illustrating that Ethereum's blob supply vastly exceeds demand and generates negligible L1 revenue.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: L2 data-availability fees are not a meaningful ETH burn/revenue source at current supply, undercutting L1 cash-flow bull cases.
insights.quote: they have so far spent about $600 purchasing that data availability.

insights.claim: The hosts predict Robinhood will not list memecoins like Cash Cat on its main app, arguing that pushing memecoins to Robinhood's ~25M-user retail base would amount to letting holders 'dump on your customers.'
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Don't bet on a Robinhood main-app memecoin listing as a catalyst; the distribution 'unlock' thesis is likely wishful.
insights.quote: All I'm hearing is Vlad, let me dump on your customers.

insights.claim: Vitalik's new Ethereum 'straw map' adds specific dates tied to hard forks on roughly a 6-9 month cadence from 2026 to 2029, organized around four north stars: fast L1 (fast finality), Tera-gas L2 (1 GB/s blobs), Giga-gas L1 (1 giga/s), and private L1.
insights.speaker: Ryan (host)
insights.so_what: Ethereum's roadmap is now concrete and dated, giving investors milestones to track through 2029.
insights.quote: The north stars are fast L1, so fast finality, uh, Terra gas L2 ... And then Gigas L1, that's one gigas per second. And then private L1.

insights.claim: Formal verification (boosted by AI) is enabling Ethereum to move from a multi-client execution layer toward a consolidated single formally-verified client, which the hosts say speeds up development by removing cross-client governance and coordination overhead.
insights.speaker: Ryan (host)
insights.so_what: Single formally-verified clients could accelerate Ethereum's shipping cadence, a structural efficiency catalyst.
insights.quote: we can consolidate have a single client as long as it's formally verified that gives us the security that we need

insights.claim: The roadmap pulls the ZK-EVM forward to the 'Kstar' hard fork (2028) from a later 'Lstar' (2029) slot, adds native rollups with a date for the first time, and accelerates quantum-resistance work from ~2029-2030 into 2028.
insights.speaker: Ryan (host)
insights.so_what: ZK-EVM and quantum timelines moving earlier are positive execution signals; native rollups gaining a date is new.
insights.quote: the ZK EVM has been sped up. Okay. Now it's in it's in Kstar the Kstar fork whereas previously it was like in LAR

insights.claim: The Ethereum roadmap plans to add privacy as a Zcash-style shielded pool at the consensus/L1 layer, effectively absorbing Zcash-like functionality natively rather than via a separate chain.
insights.speaker: Ryan (host)
insights.so_what: Native L1 privacy could pressure standalone privacy chains while strengthening ETH's store-of-value narrative.
insights.quote: what we're looking at is at least in this raw map, okay, is a a privacy pool at the consensus layer. That's like basically Zcash functionality

insights.claim: David's core thesis is that the Ethereum roadmap makes Ethereum an 'app chain' whose application is ETH itself, optimizing for ETH as a censorship-resistant, privacy-enabled store of value while trading away fast DeFi.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Reframes ETH valuation around store-of-value/monetary premium rather than fee cash flows or fast-DeFi throughput.
insights.quote: So it's it's an app chain for Ethereum.

insights.claim: David argues Ethereum fees will never become a durable value driver because the protocol scales supply while demand comes only from the app layer (DeFi summer, NFT mania), which the EF deliberately never builds, so ETH cannot be a discounted-cash-flow asset.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Rejects the ETH-as-cash-flow bull case; investors should value ETH on monetary/store-of-value terms, not fee burn.
insights.quote: fees will never be a thing. Fees will never like ... supply outstrips demand

insights.claim: The straw map raises the possibility of renewed ETH issuance reductions ('snail issuance'), flagged with an uncertainty marker, foreshadowing another contentious ETH monetary-policy debate in the community.
insights.speaker: Ryan (host)
insights.so_what: An issuance-reduction debate could become a catalyst/overhang for ETH tokenomics narrative in coming months.
insights.quote: Snail issuance ... issuance ETH issuance could be on the table in some form or another.

insights.claim: Former ETH researcher Donrad (now at Tempo) called the roadmap's 3-4 year timeline far too slow and said it should be done in one year, a pace the hosts deem unrealistic given Ethereum's decentralized culture.
insights.speaker: Ryan (host)
insights.so_what: Highlights execution-speed risk: rivals like Tempo may out-ship Ethereum's slow, coordination-heavy process.
insights.quote: But 3 to 4 years is very slow. I think we should be ambitious and get it done in one year.

insights.claim: JP Morgan's second tokenized money-market fund (ticker JTLXX) has grown ~250% in the past month to about $700 million, built on Ethereum L1 with a $1M minimum, USDC denomination, stablecoin redemptions, and designed for the Genius Act.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: The largest US bank is scaling RWA issuance on Ethereum mainnet, a credibility signal for ETH L1 as an institutional settlement venue.
insights.quote: It's actually grown 250% in the last month. So, it it is now uh a $700 million money market fund. And this is all on Ethereum.

insights.claim: JP Morgan's $700M tokenized fund has only six wallet holders on Etherscan with one wallet holding ~83%, so the hosts characterize it as an institutional accounting/parking tool rather than a broadly-held product.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Headline RWA AUM can be concentrated whale parking, not organic adoption; discount TVL that sits in a few wallets.
insights.quote: The Ether Scan has six total wallet holders of this thing.

insights.claim: Securitize (Carlos Domingo) tokenized its own post-SPAC equity first on Solana and Avalanche, not Ethereum, because Avalanche offers the permissioning/compliance and Solana the AMM liquidity/tight-slippage that regulated equity issuance needs.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Ethereum is not the automatic default for compliant tokenized equities; Solana and Avalanche are winning specific RWA use cases.
insights.quote: the fact that it's not first on Ethereum is very notable to me

insights.claim: Ethereum's slow block times prevent market makers from compliantly quoting best-execution prices for tokenized equities, a barrier Securitize cited and that the SEC is reportedly set to eliminate within a year.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: A pending SEC rule change could remove a key technical blocker to issuing tokenized equities on slower L1s like Ethereum.
insights.quote: the block times are really slow and that prevents our market makers from being able to compliantly quote the price

insights.claim: The hosts believe that even if Ethereum wins a ~60% majority of the real-world-asset tokenization war, the impact on ETH's price would be only marginal.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Tempers the RWA-adoption bull case for ETH price; RWA dominance is not a strong direct valuation driver.

insights.claim: The CME filed to run 24/7/365 markets (starting with oil) and the CFTC rejected it, a move the hosts read as the CME reacting to Hyperliquid and perp DEXes listing round-the-clock oil and gold.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Regulatory friction is protecting perp DEXes' always-on edge; a brewing commodities-exchange vs perp-DEX turf war.
insights.quote: the CME uh filed to do 24/7 365 markets ... and the CFTC just said no you can't do that

insights.claim: David frames Hyperliquid versus Lighter as this cycle's defining rivalry ('the new ETH vs Solana'), with Lighter pumping ~50% in a week after its Robinhood wallet partnership.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: A new tribal L1-style rivalry to trade; Lighter's Robinhood distribution deal is a near-term catalyst.
insights.quote: it's the new Ether for Salana. That's right.

insights.claim: Lighter is positioned as an app-specific ZK L2 using a hub-and-spoke compliance model with forward-deployed engineers, waiting on a CFTC license (its founder Vlad sits on the CFTC innovation advisory board) to serve US institutions like brokerages and Charles Schwab.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Lighter is pursuing the compliant 'Coinbase' path to the US institutional perp market versus Hyperliquid's single-venue model.
insights.quote: they are positioned to take the US market

insights.claim: David casts Lighter's verifiable ZK exchange architecture as a logical endgame not just for crypto perps but for exchange technology broadly, potentially applicable to venues like the NASDAQ and NYSE.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: If ZK exchanges become the standard, high-performance app-specific ZK L2s could disrupt incumbent exchange infrastructure.
insights.quote: a high performant appsp specific ZKL2 is such a logical conclusion of str exchange technology

insights.claim: Paradigm raised a $1.2 billion fund and will invest beyond crypto into AI, robotics, and frontier tech, which the hosts cite as evidence that the crypto-only investor is effectively dead this cycle.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Capital and mandates are broadening from 'crypto' to 'crypto + frontier tech'; pure crypto exposure is being diluted at the top.
insights.quote: there's any crypto like 100% crypto VC left. Like no one is a ... crypto only VC anymore.

insights.claim: David rotated out of ETH into smaller-cap application tokens (buying most in early-mid May, LIT on June 3 with the bulk of his sold ETH): as reported, Zcash -20%, Hype +56%, VVV -20%, Near +25%, and LIT +80% (his largest position).
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: A prominent host is publicly rotating from ETH into lower-cap, cash-flow/application tokens; LIT and Hype are his conviction winners.
insights.quote: Lit, which is a token that is my largest position, is up 80%.

insights.claim: David prefers small-cap application tokens like LIT (~$600M market cap) over ETH because ETH must justify a near-trillion-dollar valuation, making its upside a much harder fight than smaller tokens with room to grow.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Argues asymmetric upside favors smaller-cap revenue-generating apps over mega-cap ETH in this cycle.
insights.quote: when I look at like LIT that's at like a $600 million market cap, I'm like, "Oh, there's a potential large amount of growth here."

insights.claim: Ryan is still waiting for a clearer bottom (about 9.5 months into the drawdown, expecting a few more months) and cites Pump.fun's real revenue and Hyperliquid/Lighter as examples of the application-must-generate-revenue thesis guiding his shopping list.
insights.speaker: Ryan (host)
insights.so_what: Revenue-generating apps are the screening criterion for the next deployment; timing bias is patient/lower.
insights.quote: It's printing some revenue, which is insane, and that's hard to ignore if you believe in kind of like, well, an application has to deliver uh revenue
